replies.author: @ericshang7744
replies.text:
@ I’m not sure that’s true. I googled the car fire statics, and it shows NEV cars are actually safer than ICE cars in terms of fire risk. 

Another figure we can compare is there are around 174, 000 high way vehicle fires in US in 2021, so 3000 EV fires seem a big number but it’s not out of range. 40%+ car sales in China are NEV.

replies.author: @stephenwood9687
replies.text: @ericshang7744 Statistics are wonderful things to play with!  Around 70% of ICE fires are deliberate arson by thieves and insurance claimants. They are very rarely newish models. If you google China EV fires they are up 36% year on year and over 7 per day. The only ICE cars that explode like EV's do, are in Hollywood movies.

author: @lisasimpson007
text: I used to be a volunteer firefighter. I put out a gas vehicle fire with a single Type 6 engine before I went to work at my real job one morning, and before the second engine even arrived on scene. Probably a hundred gallons or less water to put it out.  What it takes to put out an electric vehicle fire isn't even in the same universe. 3000-8000 gallons directly onto the battery (per Tesla). It would have taken multiple engines plus tenders to extinguish.

author: @RichardStone-l3z
text:
This is a common problem for new design EVs made using new technology with new manufacturing methods, new software and which operate under a wide range of operating conditions. It is impossible to de-bug these new designs without years of service to learn the multiple types of failure modes that occur. I can only hope Jaguar has not scrapped all of the petrol engine manufacturing equipment. Jaguar would have been better served by providing 
hybrid petrol-electric cars to build their knowledge base and kept straight petrol engine cars in manufacturing for sale to those customers in the UK and overseas who desire to avoid electrics. This latest fiasco could hasten the demise of Jaguar, it certainly will create a significant loss of value of the company and its unfortunate shareholders. The entire situation was avoidable.

Would I buy an EV, I would go for a Tesla. Never for a Jag. It was Shaguar at some point (E-Type), now it is F...guar or Fireguar.

author: @daveindezmenez
text:
What happened to their style? And I'm not talking about their ridiculous ad. The cars look pretty ordinary and nowhere at the level of their cars from the past. They might as well be Fords.

As far as bouncing back the first thing that might make this possible is ditching the CEO and ditching their idiotic ad. Fix the issue with their EV if that is truly their direction, while keeping what is working. Finally get some car designers that will bring back the flash and panache that they once had. Will any of this happen? Probably not. Jaguar will become history with and the end of the story will be embarrassing.
